utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright you can go ahead

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hey alan i good to see you today so i looked here my appointment notes and i see that you're coming in you had some shoulder pain left shoulder pain for the last three weeks so

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: how you doing is it is it gotten any better

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah yeah i've been having a lot of pain of my shoulder for the last three weeks now and it's not getting better okay do you remember what you were doing when the pain first started

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so i i was thinking that i i ca n't recall like falling on it injuring it getting hit

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: hmmm

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i have been doing a lot of work in my basement and i even i put in a new ceiling so i do n't know if it's from all that activity doing that but otherwise that's that's all i can think of

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so do you remember hitting it or anything like that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no nothing at all

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay alright did you fall do you remember doing that

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay hmmm so like a little mystery so have you had pain in that shoulder before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i mean i'm very active so i can get pains in my shoulders but it's nothing that sometime some tylenol can help

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and are you able to move the arm or is it kinda just stuck

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i'm having a lot of pain like i can move it but you know when i try to reach for something lifting anything and even like i do n't even try to put my hands over my head because it causes so much pain

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright so does that pain radiate anywhere or like where would you say it is in your shoulder

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it actually it stays pretty much just right at the shoulder it does n't go down anywhere

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay and the pain is it is it all the time or does it come and go

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: it's pretty much all the time anytime i put any pressure on it like when i'm trying to sleep it hurts even more so it's been affecting my sleep as well

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so i know you mentioned tylenol so this time i have n't taken anything for it

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah i i do the tylenol which usually works for me and it does take the edge off but i still have pain

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay did you try icing it at all

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i iced it initially but i have n't iced it at all recently

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: alright

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and so with your shoulder have you experienced any numbness in your arm or in your fingers

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: no numbness or tingling

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay good so i'm gon na go ahead and do a quick physical exam and take a look at your your shoulder so i reviewed your your vitals everything looks good with that so touch here in your shoulder so your left shoulder exam you have limited active and passive range of motion so pressure here so that there is tenderness of the greater

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: tuberosity of the humerus let's see there is no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acro

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: acromioclavicular joints

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yeah yeah

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and looks like you have good hand grip let me see so on the neurovascular exam of your left arm your capillary refill is less than three seconds and your sensation is is intact to light touch

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: yes thank you yep

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: so you did get a we get we had to get a x-ray of your shoulder before you came in and so it's normal so that's really good so there is no fractures no bony abnormalities so let's talk a little bit about my assessment and plan for you so you you do have that left shoulder pain so your symptoms are

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: most likely due to a rotator cuff tendinopathy so this means that you injured tendon you have injured tendons and muscles that make up your shoulder and make up your shoulder muscles so what i'm gon na do is i'm gon na order an mri of your left shoulder

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: and so we're gon na begin with that just to make sure nothing else is going on have you done physical therapy before

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: i have n't

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: okay so what i'm gon na do i'm going to refer you to physical therapy for approximately six to eight weeks and so they can help you strengthen those muscles around your shoulder and that should definitely help with the pain during that time you can also continue to take tylenol i do n't think i need to prescribe anything else for the pain you said as it's working pretty good for you so if your symptoms do n't improve we can consider a steroid injection of your shoulder which should provide some relief but i think right now we can just go with the the pt and hopefully that works to alleviate your injury so do you have any questions about the plan

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: so like i said i'm really active do you think that this pain will ever go away

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: yeah so many patients are very successful with rehab and so we'll start with that and see how you do most most of the time once we build up those muscles around that shoulder you know things things the pain alleviates itself and and and you will be good to go back to working on your basement and running and jogging and lifting weights all all the active things people do these days

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay alright thank you

utterances.speaker: doctor
utterances.text: bye

utterances.speaker: patient
utterances.text: okay bye

detail_list.summary: Alan Mitchell is a 69-year-old male presenting with left shoulder pain for three weeks.
The doctor asks if the patient's shoulder pain has improved.
The patient reports persistent left shoulder pain for three weeks.
The patient denies any specific injury, fall, or trauma to the shoulder.
The patient reports recent strenuous activity involving basement renovations and ceiling installation.
The doctor asks if the patient remembers hitting the shoulder.
The doctor asks if the patient remembers falling.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced similar shoulder pain in the past.
The patient reports a history of shoulder pain that typically resolves with Tylenol.
The doctor asks about the patient's ability to move the arm.
The patient reports pain with reaching, lifting, and overhead activities.
The doctor asks about the location and radiation of the pain.
The patient reports the pain is localized to the shoulder and does not radiate.
The doctor asks about the frequency of the pain.
The patient reports constant pain that worsens at night and disrupts sleep.
The patient reports using Tylenol for partial pain relief.
The doctor asks if the patient has used ice for the pain.
The patient reports using ice initially but not recently.
The doctor asks if the patient has experienced numbness or tingling in the arm or fingers.
The patient denies numbness or tingling.
The doctor notes normal vital signs, limited active and passive range of motion, and tenderness over the greater tuberosity of the humerus.
The doctor notes no tenderness at the sternoclavicular or acromioclavicular joints.
The doctor notes good hand grip, capillary refill less than three seconds, and intact sensation to light touch.
The doctor reports normal shoulder X-rays with no fractures or bony abnormalities.
Rotator cuff tendinopathy
The doctor orders an MRI of the left shoulder.
The doctor asks if the patient has previously undergone physical therapy.
The doctor refers the patient to physical therapy for 6-8 weeks and advises continued use of Tylenol as needed.
The patient asks about the prognosis for recovery.
The doctor explains that physical therapy is generally successful for strengthening the shoulder and alleviating pain.
